Hed: AI Helped Me See My Work More Clearly
Dek: A writer uses a retrospective to understand where her effort creates the most value

<br />

***

<br />

A thing to know about me is that I assume every strong week is a fluke.

So when I finally asked an AI system to help me review a year of work, I expected a prettier version of the same vague reassurance I had always gotten from performance reviews. I did not expect it to change how I thought about my work.

It started as a routine planning exercise. I pulled quarterly numbers for the writing projects I had touched, exported notes from our CMS, and asked for patterns. Then I kept going. I compared output, response quality, and follow-on engagement. By the time I stopped, I had built a case for what kind of work I should keep doing and what kind I should hand off.

The most useful part was not the scorekeeping. It was the specificity. The essays where I took a real position outperformed the polite summaries. The pieces with a sharp point of view drew stronger responses than the ones where I tried to sound broadly correct. The writing I had dismissed as too personal or too narrow often turned out to be the work readers remembered.

That shifted the question. Instead of asking, "Am I safe?" I could ask, "What should I do next?"

The answer was not "do more of everything." It was narrower than that. Do more of the work only I can do. Use the data to notice patterns, then use judgment to decide which patterns matter.

That is what the AI helped with. It did not tell me who I was. It helped me see what was already there.
